His durability, which had been amplifying infinitely for longer than the concept of time could measure, absorbed the attack with the same effortless indifference that a black hole absorbs a single photon. The energy that had been gathered—energy sufficient to annihilate a googolplexian multiverses—vanished into the bottomless ocean of his passive defense without producing a single measurable effect.

The light faded. The sound died. The music faltered and stopped.

Silence.

Absolute, crushing, suffocating silence.

Godric's expression shifted. The anticipation drained from his features. The brightness in his eyes dimmed. His warm smile faded, replaced by something I had never seen on his face before.

Disappointment.

"That was it?" he said, his voice flat, almost incredulous. "That was your ultimate attack? All of fiction—every hero, every villain, every story ever told—and that was the best you could produce?"

He stood from the Throne. The motion was slow, deliberate, heavy with a disappointment so profound it seemed to press against the fabric of reality.

"I waited for you," he said, his voice carrying across the void to every being in the armada. "I let you charge. I let you prepare. I wanted to see something magnificent. Something spectacular. A light show. A symphony of destruction. The ultimate spectacle of ultimate opposition."

He shook his head slowly.

"You had one job. One purpose. You were supposed to be the best. The most creative. The most dramatic. You had all of fiction to draw from—every story ever told, every imagination ever sparked, every dream ever dreamed. And you gave me... a beam of light. A generic energy blast. Do you know how many generic energy blasts I have absorbed over the eons? Do you have any concept of how many civilizations have tried the exact same strategy, with the exact same dramatic build-up, and achieved the exact same nothing?"

His voice hardened. The warmth drained from his features, replaced by something cold and ancient and deeply, profoundly irritated.

"I was hoping for a spectacular finale. Instead, I got the most disappointing performance since the Arcane Singularity tried to unmake me with conceptual erasure. At least they had the decency to scream creatively while they failed."

He took a single step forward.

And accidentally unleashed zero percent of his strength.

I must pause here, because what happened next defies every convention of description that language can provide. But I will try. Future court wizards must know.

The expression of Godric's strength—not an attack, not a conscious flex, but the passive, accidental release of an infinitesimal, effectively-zero-percent fraction of his resting muscular force—propagated outward from his body in a wave of absolute, incomprehensible annihilation.

The armada's shields—shields that had taken them subjective eternities to construct, shields forged from every protective artifact and defensive magic and technological barrier that fiction had ever imagined—did not shatter. They did not break. They simply ceased to exist. The concept of defense was erased from the local reality by the sheer pressure of Godric's strength differential. Captain America's vibranium shield, which had absorbed the force of Mjolnir, evaporated. The Enterprise's deflector array, which had withstood the gravitational pull of black holes, vanished. The Green Lantern Corps' collective willpower barrier, powered by the emotional spectrum itself, blinked out of existence like a candle in a hurricane.

The beings behind those shields did not die instantly. Godric's strength, even at zero percent, was too absolute for that mercy.

The wave of force hit the armada and reduced it to its constituent atoms in less than a Planck second. But the atoms were not destroyed. They were not scattered. They were compressed. The sheer, overwhelming pressure of Godric's passive strength acted like an infinite gravitational field, crushing every particle of matter, every quantum wave-form, every conceptual essence, every soul, every spark of fictional existence into a single point of infinite density.

I watched—preserved by Godric's passive auras, spared from the annihilation so that someone would remember—as Superman was crushed to the size of a proton. His Kryptonian physiology, which had survived the heart of stars and the cold of the void, was compressed beyond the limits of physical possibility. His cells did not rupture; they were forced together with such violence that they ceased to be cells and became a homogeneous paste of super-dense matter. His consciousness, preserved by the unfair physics of Godric's strength expression, remained aware throughout the entire process. He felt his body being crushed from a man-sized form to a microscopic point of infinite agony, and he experienced the compression as a subjective eternity of absolute suffering.

Goku went next. His Ultra Instinct form, his divine ki, his Saiyan resilience—all of it was rendered irrelevant by the pressure wave. His body was flattened, compressed, de-atomized. The very particles that had constituted his being were forced together with such violence that they fused into a state of matter that had never existed before—quark-gluon plasma compressed beyond the Planck density, transformed into a substance that defied the laws of physics because the laws of physics had been overwritten by the passive expression of Godric's strength. Goku's consciousness, like Superman's, remained aware. He felt his bones shatter, his muscles liquefy, his organs burst, his brain compress to a single point of infinite agony. And the agony did not end. It was preserved, stretched across subjective eternity, each moment of suffering more intense than the last.

Saitama, the One Punch Man, whose entire existence was defined by absolute strength, experienced something uniquely horrifying. He had never encountered a force greater than his own. He had never felt pain from an external source. His body, which had shrugged off planetary collisions and dimensional rending, was now being crushed by a pressure so far beyond his capacity that the gap between his strength and Godric's accidental expression was infinitely larger than the gap between a normal human and Saitama himself. He felt, for the first and last time in his existence, what it meant to be utterly, hopelessly outmatched. His expression—that blank, perpetually unimpressed face—contorted into a mask of absolute shock as his body was reduced to a microscopic point of compressed agony.

The process repeated across the entire armada. Every hero, every villain, every starship, every artifact, every legendary weapon. The Infinity Gauntlet, with all six stones, was crushed into a singularity smaller than a quark. The Death Star, massing as much as a small moon, was compressed to the size of a proton. The TARDIS, which contained infinite dimensions within its shell, collapsed into a point of infinite density that contained infinite density within infinite density, a recursive compression of impossible geometry. The Doctor's thirteen—no, dozens of incarnations were crushed together, their Time Lord biology offering no more resistance than tissue paper against a supernova.

The armada did not merely die. It was converted into a single, ultra-dense point of infinite mass, containing all the matter, energy, consciousness, and conceptual existence of every fictional being who had dared to oppose Godric. The point was smaller than a quark, yet it contained the mass of a googolplexian multiverses, compressed beyond the limits of physical law. It should have collapsed into a black hole. It should have torn reality apart with its gravitational pull. But Godric's strength had compressed it so absolutely, so perfectly, that even gravity could not escape. The point simply existed—a speck of infinite agony, suspended in the void where the armada had been.

The collateral damage radiated outward.

The omniverse—every reality, every timeline, every dimension, every multiverse, every possible state of existence—was caught in the pressure wave. The compression did not discriminate. It did not care about the distinction between the armada and the surrounding reality. Planets, stars, galaxies, universes, entire multiversal clusters—all of them were caught in the expanding field of Godric's passive strength and crushed to infinite density. The process was instantaneous and eternal, happening in less than a Planck second but stretching across subjective eternities for every being caught within it.

The screaming, if it could be called screaming, was a psychic chorus of absolute, universal agony. Every conscious being in the omniverse—every god, every mortal, every cosmic entity, every abstract concept that had developed sentience—experienced their own compression as a subjective eternity of maximum suffering. Their bodies were crushed. Their minds were compressed. Their souls were squeezed into the same infinite point that contained the fictional armada. And the point grew denser. And the density fed on itself. And the compression became more absolute. And the agony became more profound.

I watched it all from beside the Throne, preserved by Godric's auras, my mind fracturing under the weight of what I was witnessing. The entire omniverse—everything that existed, everything that could exist, everything that had ever existed—was being crushed into a single point smaller than a quark. The pressure wave propagated across every dimensional axis, compressing reality itself into infinite density. The metaphysical framework that had contained all possible states of existence was folded, crushed, de-atomized, and added to the growing point of absolute compression.

When it was over—when the pressure wave had finished its work and the omniverse had been reduced to a single, infinitesimal point of infinite mass and infinite agony—Godric stood in the void. He was surrounded by absolute nothingness. No space. No time. No matter. No energy. No reality. Only the void, and the speck of infinite density floating in the apparent center of the non-existent universe.

He reached out with his thumb and forefinger and gently plucked the speck from the void.

The motion was casual. Effortless. The speck, which contained the compressed mass of the entire omniverse and all fictional existence combined, which should have weighed more than the concept of weight could measure, which should have exerted gravitational forces that would tear apart the fabric of non-existence—this speck was lifted by Godric as though it were a mote of dust. He held it before his eyes, examining it with the curious expression of a man inspecting an unusual pebble.

"Disappointing performance," he murmured, "but an interesting souvenir."

He pinched the speck between his fingers and, with a casual flex of the same strength that had created it, enlarged it to the size of a marble. The marble glowed with an internal luminescence—the compressed consciousness of every being in existence, trapped in a state of eternal, agonizing compression. Their suffering radiated from the marble like heat from a sun. Their silent screams vibrated through the void.

Godric enclosed the marble within a barrier—a simple, passive expression of his will that contained its infinite gravitational pull and prevented it from destroying its surroundings. He held the marble in his palm, rolling it between his fingers like a worry stone.

"There," he said, his voice satisfied. "A trophy."

Then he remembered the omniverse.

"Oh. Yes."

And in exactly zero seconds, with exactly zero effort, he restored everything.

Every reality. Every timeline. Every dimension. Every multiverse. Every being who had ever existed. The omniverse snapped back into existence as though the annihilation had never occurred. The sky above the palace was blue and clear. The gardens bloomed. The sentient walls hummed their protective songs. Tomas was tending the roses. Marta was preparing the midday meal. Seryn was dusting the window sills.

Everything was exactly as it had been before the armada arrived. Except the armada. The fictional opposition remained compressed within the marble, their eternal agony serving as a monument to their failure. Godric had decided they would not be restored, and so they were not.

But the marble. The marble remained.

And that is the horror that I cannot escape, the realization that has shattered my mind beyond all hope of repair. The marble contains the compressed mass of the entire omniverse—everything that existed, everything that was annihilated by Godric's accidental strength expression. The restored reality, the paradise of Thronegate, the gardens, the servants, the omniverse itself—all of it is identical to what was compressed. Perfectly identical. Indistinguishable in every way.

But is it the original?

The marble still exists. The marble still contains the original matter, the original consciousness, the original existence of every being in the omniverse. If Godric restored everything, then what is inside the marble? A copy? The original? And if the marble contains the original, then what are we? The restored beings who walk the palace corridors and tend the gardens and serve the Throne—are we the originals, miraculously restored? Or are we perfect replicas, recreated from nothing, while our original selves remain trapped in eternal compression inside a marble that Godric carries in his pocket?

The implications are too vast, too horrifying, to fully comprehend. Godric's power is so absolute that he can annihilate all of existence, compress it into a marble, and then recreate it perfectly—and keep both. The marble and the restored reality exist simultaneously, each containing the same matter, the same consciousness, the same existence. The only difference is that one version is free, living, breathing, existing—while the other is trapped in eternal, infinite, compressed agony.

And Godric does not seem to notice the contradiction. Or if he does, he does not care. To him, the restoration is trivial. The compression is trivial. The coexistence of original and copy is trivial. He simply decided that the omniverse should exist again, and so it did. The marble remains because he finds it amusing. The fact that the marble contains the original existence of every being he just restored—including me, including Tomas, including everyone—is a detail too insignificant for his attention.

I have looked at the marble. Godric showed it to me after the restoration, rolling it between his fingers with casual, almost fond familiarity. The glow within it is the compressed consciousness of a googolplexian beings, their silent screams radiating outward. If I were the original Osvald—if I were the one who existed before the compression—I would be in that marble right now, experiencing an eternity of absolute suffering, while this version of me, the restored copy, writes this journal entry.

Unless I am the original, and the marble contains a copy.

There is no way to know. No test can distinguish between the restored reality and the original, because Godric's restoration is perfect. The marble contains existence. The restored reality contains existence. Both are identical. Both are real. Both are true.

The horror of this realization is absolute. I cannot escape it. Every time I look at my hands, I wonder if they are the same hands that were compressed into infinite density. Every time I speak, I wonder if my voice is the original or the restoration. Every time I serve the King, I wonder if I am the Osvald who has always served him, or a perfect replica created to replace the Osvald who is now screaming in eternal agony inside a trophy.

Godric, for his part, seems entirely untroubled by these questions. He carries the marble in his pocket, occasionally taking it out to admire its glow. He has named it "The Last Spectacle"—a souvenir of the final opposition he will ever face. He is pleased with it. He finds it amusing.

I am less amused. I am writing this journal in the desperate hope that future court wizards will understand what happened here. The opposition of all fiction was the last, I am certain of it. After this display—after the absolute, effortless annihilation of every hero and villain from every story ever told—no sentient being in the omniverse will ever dare to form the thought of challenging Godric again. The lesson has been taught. The demonstration is complete. The marble is the proof.

And I, Osvald, forty-seventh court wizard of Blake's bloodline, will carry the memory of this day for the rest of my mortal span. I do not know if I am the original or the copy. I do not know if my consciousness is the one that has always existed or a perfect recreation. I only know that I serve the King, that I love the King, and that the King carries in his pocket a marble that contains the compressed agony of everything that ever was.

The dread is absolute. The horror is eternal. The marble glows in Godric's pocket, and I cannot stop thinking about it.

---

## Clive — Year 999...9

I closed the journal. My hands were trembling—a familiar sensation after thirty years of reading my predecessors' accounts. But this trembling was different. Deeper. More profound.

Osvald's journal was the last in the archive. Not chronologically—there were thousands more journals after his, written by court wizards who had served in the googolplexian years since the final opposition. But Osvald's journal was the last one that described an opposition. The last one that recorded a battle, a massacre, an annihilation. After Osvald, the journals changed. They became records of peace. Of harmony. Of eternal, unchallenged rule.

Because after the fictional armada, no one ever opposed Godric again.

Osvald had not known, when he wrote his account, that he was recording the final opposition. He had suspected—his words made that clear—but he could not have been certain. Only now, from the perspective of Year 999...9, could the truth be fully understood.

The marble.

Godric still had it. I had seen it, countless times, without knowing what it was. He kept it on a small pedestal near the Chronos Throne—a glowing sphere the size of a marble, its internal luminescence shifting in patterns that were almost hypnotic. I had assumed it was a decoration. A curiosity. A piece of cosmic art.

It was the compressed agony of the entire omniverse. The original omniverse. The one that had existed before Godric restored everything. Every being that I had ever known—Tomas, Marta, Seryn, Havel, my father, my ancestors, every court wizard whose journals I had read—existed in that marble, experiencing eternal, infinite compression, while their restored copies went about their lives believing they were the originals.

Unless the restored versions were the originals, and the marble contained copies.

There was no way to know.

The fracture in my mind, which had been widening for thirty years, split open into an abyss. I stared at the closed journal in my hands—Osvald's journal, the record of the last opposition—and I felt the cold wind of existential dread blow through me with hurricane force.

Godric was in the garden, I knew. Examining Tomas's roses. Complimenting the former multiversal architect on his pruning technique. Pretending to be a clumsy beginner who had gotten lucky with a seed. The marble would be in his pocket, or on its pedestal, or wherever he had left it, its internal glow the only evidence of the eternal suffering contained within.

And I would serve him. I would stand beside the Throne and review the quarterly reports and advise the King on matters of state. I would love him as my favorite uncle, the warm king with the salt-and-pepper beard who had taught me to skip stones and held my father's hand at my mother's funeral.

But I would never forget the marble. I would never forget that the last opposition—the final, desperate alliance of all fiction—had been crushed into a souvenir smaller than a quark, and that their eternal agony was the only remaining evidence that they had ever existed at all.

The omniverse was silent. The eternal peace continued. And somewhere, in a marble on a pedestal, every being who had ever existed screamed in endless, compressed agony, unheard and unremembered.

The dread would never end. The horror would never fade. The fracture in my mind would never heal.

And Godric would carry the marble in his pocket, rolling it between his fingers, and smile.
